items.question: Define generalization in supervised learning.
items.answer: Generalization is a model's ability to perform well on unseen (x, y) from the same problem — accurate predictions on new examples not used during training, rather than memorizing the training set.

items.question: What is the generalization gap and how do you interpret a large one?
items.answer: Generalization gap = test (or validation) error minus train error. A large gap with low train error suggests overfitting — the model fits training data well but fails on unseen examples.

items.question: Why is training accuracy a poor proxy for production performance?
items.answer: Training metrics are computed on data used in fit(); flexible models can memorize noise and achieve high training accuracy while generalizing poorly to held-out or live data.

items.question: How do hold-out test sets estimate generalization?
items.answer: Fit the model on training data only, run predict on held-out labeled test rows never used in fit(), and compute metrics comparing predictions to y_test — mimicking inference on unseen x with labels available only for evaluation.

items.question: What is the i.i.d. assumption and why does it matter for generalization?
items.answer: Training and future examples are assumed independent and identically distributed from the same process. Generalization estimates and guarantees rely on this — distribution shift breaks the assumption and degrades real-world performance.

items.question: What is data leakage and how does it affect generalization estimates?
items.answer: Leakage occurs when information from validation/test data influences training (e.g. fitting a scaler on the full dataset). Held-out rows are no longer truly unseen, so reported metrics overstate generalization.
